name: Study 1: Playing Without a Plan = Losing

comments.text:
This game was sent by one of my subscribers. The players are rated around 800. 

Black has proposed a queen trade.

In this position, White didn't accept the trade, but in a careless way. This first sequence shows how White played without a plan.
This position is equal because while it is true that Black will lose some pawns, White will also lose pawns because they're so weak.
White took.
The king started going towards the h-file.

This move was crushing. 

White would have to play another bad waiting move. They're in Zugzwang.
White pushed.

Black recaptured.

Now, it was time for White to activate the king.

White didn't miss checkmate in one again. 

Truly, a really crazy endgame that White had no business winning, but Black allowed themselves to lose by playing without a plan.

And Black blundered a pawn. 

The pawn could be captured here, easily, by either the c- or a-pawn.

This wasn't forced at all.
But White just took the dangerous pawn.
Black castled queenside.
Even though White was there to defend...
The king could go down.
Black played ...Rd7, trying to control the 7th rank.
White castled.

White got the opposition. 

This was completely unnecessary. 

White needs to get to the key squares, not fight for the opposition here.
Black recaptured.
If you don't know if you should trade or not, you should just develop.
If f3...
A potential idea was to take the knight somewhere different.
White thanked Black for getting them out of Zugzwang.
White entered the open g-file.
The king went back to d5.
The king went down.
White began to get their king involved.

Instead of taking one of the pawns, White gave a check on the 8th rank. 

This wasn't consistent with White's Rg6 move.

Still, even though it wasn't a threat, I don't think the trade should have been provoked like this. 

The pin wasn't that big of a deal.
Black went up the board.

And White moved the king sideways, staying on the 1st rank. 

This wasn't the best approach. Even with only two available moves, there's a difference between one or the other.
Black pushed, trying to maybe catch White off guard.

And Black challenged White's control. 

So it seems both sides have some idea of how to use their rooks, placing them on open files.

Black's pawns are simply stronger.

Black could try using their better structure to create a passed pawn.

The point is that the kings will create a passed pawn and both sides will go for a pawn race.

Nonetheless, even though both sides queen, Black will have an advantage.
The king came back down.
The point is that many trades occur, and both sides just simplify.

The way things turned out, White traded into an endgame in which they're slightly worse. 

Those doubled isolated pawn islands never went away.
This blocks the tension between the queens.

Black took on d4. 

But the problem is White can now consolidate with c3+.

White recaptured, controlling the g-file. 

A self-inflicted wound by Black.
The king went to h3.
Black cannot defend.
White went after the pawns.
White went forward.

And the bishop pinned the knight. 

However, ...Bxf6 isn't much of a threat. I liked ...Rhf8 to recapture with a rook and always aim at the doubled f-pawns.
The reason is that you'll take the queenside pawns since White would have to go after the f-pawn!
But the pawn will queen.

Black played ...d6, attacking the knight. 

...d6 is the most obvious move after the knight lands in a central square.

But the point is you've got some pressure against d6. 

If ...Ke7, there's Ba3.
And White was lucky that they were still winning after this tunnel vision towards the kingside.
The king went down, trying to defend.
Black took.
And when the king went to h3...
White activated their rook, but the pressure isn't significant.

White found the move. 

This was revolutionary. All other legal moves were losing.
All of White's legal moves lose material.
Black went to f6 with their knight.
White came closer.

White has the same problem: they don't have useful moves. 

After f3, White is lost. Zugzwang can be put on the board once again with ...a6.
Black took the pawn.
White began to push.
Black played ...a6 to try to ward off the pawns.
White took the pawn, improving their structure.

Going towards the key squares! 

White's king is already better.
White recaptured, and the Ne5 back to f3 operation has resulted in them getting doubled isolated pawns for the endgame.

White played a3.

There's no purpose to a3. I don't care if the engine says it's fine and that the evaluation doesn't change. 

Why was this played? If you cannot give a good answer to that, then it shouldn't have been played.
Pushing.
White followed.
The king went after the h5-pawn.
But, Black went for a pawn trade, giving White more moves and freeing their position.
Black went after the c-pawn.

And Black attacked the f3-knight. 

I'd consider this a positional threat because if ...Bxf3, White would get doubled pawns...again.
The king went to e4, but the pawn is defended and it can run.
White had to go for a pawn race.
And now it's White who's winning!
There are no stalemates.
The king tried coming around.
Black will have a legal move.

White played a waiting move, since all their moves are waiting moves at this point. 

The pawns are doubled and cannot make progress.
Sure, Black could still trade.
And Black recaptured.
And White just came right back.
White pushed.
Splitting the pawns is actually good now.
White followed. White should have gone to this square originally, anticipating Black's defensive plan. But, it's still winning.
But it doesn't stop the c-pawn!
White waited one more time.
White went after the pawns.
The king came up. Only legal move.
Black took on f3.
And Black reached the c3-pawn.
The king went up.

White played Kh1. 

Moves like Kh1 are logical when you want to push the g-pawn and attack on the g-file, or get the king out of the diagonal because there's a dark-squared bishop aiming at f2. It makes no sense here.
Black went to c5.

This move gets the king closer to the center, if you want to go there, or to the kingside, if you want to go there as well! 

You have to go for multipurpose moves.
White promoted.
And this is crushing!

Despite this, White doesn't have any pawn breaks. 

Black is still winning.
The king goes to the corner.
But it was too slow.
White missed checkmate in one.
And Black went back.
So Black would have to block their own bishop in to defend.
White gave a check.

Having run out of waiting moves, White sacrificed a pawn. 

However, this shouldn't be taken.

And White wasn't so time efficient in their conversion. 

The c-pawn should have been the focus.
The reason you play Rg6 is to take the pawns, not to give a check on g8.
Black went to b6.
White gave another check.

The a-pawn is a problem for White now. 

This is completely winning for Black.
White got closer, trying to defend.
The pawn was passed.

In the game, Black took. 

The problem with this is that it destroys the structure and makes the pawns weaker.
Check.
Black got their trade in all the same. So if there wasn't an idea behind Ne5, it shouldn't have been played!
If the pawn goes up...
And Black continued improving their pawns, making progress and getting closer.

Black got their king closer to the action, going after the weak pawns. 

White should have kept the rooks on the board to take some of Black's pawns.
And now we've got two islands of doubled isolated pawns.
White controlled the file, restricting the king.

Black played ...e5, controlling more dark squares. 

Without a dark-squared bishop, this is sound strategy.

Black followed up a3, a fairly ineffective move, with giving their opponent the g-file.

If you initiate a trade of rooks you give up the file on which you traded the rooks.
This should be a trivial win for White.
Black cannot do much.
